summary: I was born in Kazakhstan and lived there for the first three years of my life before moving to Syria. Although I don’t have many memories from there, it’s the place where my life began. Kazakhstan is part of my early identity even though my upbringing mostly happened elsewhere.

I was raised in Syria after moving there from Kazakhstan when I was very young. Growing up in Syria was full of memories with my family and sisters. However, my childhood was deeply affected by the Syrian civil war, especially since we lived near the Russian Embassy in Damascus, which became a dangerous place during the conflict.

I was eleven years old when the Syrian civil war started, and at first, we thought it would be over quickly. It began with protests after a young man was tortured for graffiti against the president, and then it escalated into violence that eventually reached my neighborhood. Living next to the Russian Embassy, we experienced bombings and gunfire firsthand, which forced me to grow up fast and rely on my faith and community to survive.

Our home was right next to the Russian Embassy in Syria, which made living there very dangerous during the civil war. The embassy was a target for bombings, and one bomb even exploded just a floor below us by mistake. The conflict around this area was intense, with gunshots and explosions happening nearby, making it a frightening place to live.

When describing the spread of the Syrian civil war, I compared it to living in Dallas while the conflict started far away, like in Austin or Houston. This comparison helped me explain how the violence gradually moved closer to where I lived. It gave a sense of distance and progression of the war’s impact on my life.
cues: comparison of Syria to Texas cities
conflict starting far and moving closer

summary: My family and I had to resettle in the United States to escape the dangers of the Syrian civil war. The process was difficult, especially since my older sister had to go to Dubai alone before joining us because she didn’t have her own immigration case. Living in the U.S. now is part of building a new life after all the trauma we endured in Syria.
cues: older sister went to Dubai first

summary: My older sister had to move to Dubai by herself because she couldn’t come directly to the United States with us. She stayed there for some time before eventually joining us, but it was hard because our youngest sister has never even met her. Dubai was an important but difficult stop in our family’s journey.

The COVID-19 pandemic added another layer of difficulty to our lives, especially with travel restrictions that prevented my youngest sister from coming to the U.S. for my graduation. It also reminded me of how communities face challenges together, much like during the war, but with divisions still present. The pandemic has affected how we connect and support each other during these times.

My faith in Islam played a big role in helping me and my family get through the hardships of the war and displacement. Believing in God gave me hope and strength to stay calm during frightening moments, like the bomb explosion near our home. Islam has been a source of comfort and resilience throughout my life’s challenges.
